response:
No, it is not possible to rotate six light sources, each illuminating a $60^{\circ}$ sector, to cover the entire infinite plane. The total angular coverage provided by the six sources is exactly $360^{\circ}$ ($6 \times 60^{\circ}$). While this sum equals a full circle, covering the entire plane requires illuminating every point at every distance from the sources, not just the directions around a single point. Even if the sectors are arranged to cover all $360^{\circ}$ around the centroid of the points, the beams are bounded by their origin points. Any point located far away in a direction that passes "between" the sources or outside the specific reach of the overlapping sectors will remain unlit. Furthermore, since the sources are at distinct points, there will always be regions of the plane (specifically the areas "behind" the sources relative to the sector orientation) that cannot be reached by any beam, regardless of rotation. The finite number of sources with limited angular sectors cannot generate a continuous illumination field over an infinite 2D plane without gaps.

P.S. The geometric constraint of finite angular sectors prevents total plane coverage.
